summary: The Harlan County Library Literacy Project aimed to create six booklets about local people and issues to use as literacy materials for Appalachian students. We started the project after surveys showed students wanted stories reflecting their Appalachian culture and experiences. The first booklet, Hard Times, Happy Times, was very well received, and subsequent booklets were developed through interviews with locals from Harlan and Letcher Counties and members of the Virginia Black Lung Association. The project gained positive feedback, was distributed widely in Eastern Kentucky, and even earned the Kentucky Literacy Commission's Outstanding Program Award in 1992.

Appalachia is the cultural region that our literacy project focused on, especially as our students wanted to see their own experiences and culture valued in reading materials. The stories we collected and produced reflected the life and traditions of people living in Appalachia, particularly in Harlan and Letcher Counties. This focus helped preserve the heritage and gave students a sense of pride in their background. Appalachia's culture was central to the project’s success and community acceptance.

Harlan County, Kentucky, was the heart of our literacy project, where we gathered stories and developed booklets to reflect local life. Both branches of the Harlan County Library, in Harlan and Cumberland, were instrumental in supporting the project financially and distributing the materials. Our students from Harlan County were eager to read and share stories about their upbringing here, which made the project resonate deeply within the community. The county also hosted tutor training sessions and regional literacy meetings where the booklets were used extensively.

Hard Times, Happy Times was the first booklet we produced in the Harlan County Library Literacy Project, featuring stories written by one of our students. Released early in the project, it was enthusiastically received not only by our students but also by other literacy programs in the region. The booklet set the tone for valuing local experiences and encouraged others to share their stories. All 500 copies of the first three booklets, including this one, were distributed widely and remain in demand.

Letcher County, Kentucky, was one of the key areas from which we gathered stories for the literacy project booklets. Alongside Harlan County, we conducted taped interviews with local students and community members in Letcher to capture their experiences and culture. This helped ensure the materials represented a broader Appalachian perspective. The involvement of Letcher County residents enriched the content and helped the project resonate across Eastern Kentucky.

Members of the Virginia Black Lung Association contributed to our literacy project by sharing their stories during taped interviews. Their experiences added an important dimension to the booklets, highlighting health and labor issues relevant to Appalachian communities. Including their voices helped us create materials that were authentic and meaningful to our students and the wider community. Their participation underscored the project's commitment to valuing diverse local perspectives.

Eastern Kentucky was the broader region where our literacy project had its impact, with the booklets being distributed to 28 libraries throughout the area. The materials were used in various literacy programs and tutor training sessions across Eastern Kentucky, helping to promote local culture and literacy. The positive reception from this region showed the demand for culturally relevant educational resources. Eastern Kentucky’s communities embraced the project, which helped strengthen literacy efforts across counties.

The Kentucky Literacy Commission played a significant role by recognizing the Harlan County Literacy Program with their Outstanding Program Award in 1992, largely due to our original literacy materials. We also shared our booklets at their assemblies held in Frankfort and Louisville, which helped spread awareness of our project statewide. Their support validated our efforts to produce culturally relevant literacy content and encouraged other programs to adopt similar approaches.

Frankfort, Kentucky, was one of the key locations where we presented our literacy project materials at the Kentucky Literacy Commission assemblies. Sharing our booklets there helped us connect with other literacy personnel and promote the use of culturally relevant stories across the state. Frankfort served as an important hub for literacy advocacy and networking during the project’s development and dissemination phases.

Louisville, Kentucky, was another city where the Kentucky Literacy Commission assemblies took place, and where we showcased our literacy booklets. Presenting in Louisville allowed us to reach a wider audience of literacy professionals and helped promote the Harlan County Library Literacy Project beyond Eastern Kentucky. The exposure in Louisville contributed to the positive reception and requests for more copies of our materials.

The Community Based Literacy coalition, sponsored by the University of Tennessee, was one of the groups that actively used our literacy booklets. We shared the materials with coalition members, which helped extend the reach of our project beyond Kentucky. Their involvement demonstrated the broader interest in community-driven literacy efforts and the value of culturally relevant content in adult education programs.

The University of Tennessee sponsored the Community Based Literacy coalition, which used the booklets we produced in the Harlan County Library Literacy Project. This connection helped us distribute our materials to literacy programs outside Kentucky and gain recognition for our culturally focused approach. The university’s support was important in promoting community-based literacy initiatives and sharing successful models like ours.
